modules.title: Argumentative essay
modules.html:
An argumentative essay presents a position on a topic and defends it with reasons and evidence. It has a clear thesis, gives well-organised arguments, considers counter-arguments, and ends with a firm conclusion.

Layout

  1. Introduction — background + thesis statement + your stand.
  2. Body — one argument per paragraph, each with a topic sentence, evidence and explanation.
  3. Counter-argument — briefly present the other side and refute it.
  4. Conclusion — restate the thesis; summarise reasons; issue a call to action.

Use linking words: firstly, in addition, moreover, however, on the other hand, in conclusion. Keep the tone firm but polite; avoid slang.

examples.q: State a thesis for the topic 'Boarding schools are better than day schools'.
examples.a: e.g. I strongly support the view that boarding schools are better than day schools because they build discipline, independence and academic focus.

modules.title: Expository essay
modules.html:
An expository essay explains, defines or informs. It is factual, objective and orderly.

Common patterns

  • Process: how something is done (steps in order).
  • Cause and effect: reasons and their results.
  • Comparison and contrast: similarities and differences.
  • Classification: dividing into categories.
  • Definition: what a term means, with examples.

Tips

  • Begin with a clear thesis: what will be explained.
  • Support each point with facts or examples.
  • Use transitions to move from one point to the next.

examples.q: Suggest a thesis for the essay 'How to prepare for an examination'.
examples.a: e.g. Effective examination preparation involves planning early, mastering the syllabus, practising past questions and staying healthy.

modules.title: Formal and semi-formal letters
modules.html:
Formal letters follow a strict layout; semi-formal letters (to teachers, older acquaintances) relax it slightly.

Formal letter parts

  1. Writer's address — top right.
  2. Date — below writer's address.
  3. Recipient's address — top left.
  4. Salutation — Dear Sir / Madam, or Dear Mr Adamu,
  5. Subject / Heading — a short line stating the purpose (often underlined).
  6. Body — introduction, details, conclusion.
  7. Complimentary close — Yours faithfully, (with Dear Sir/Madam) or Yours sincerely, (with Dear Mr X).
  8. Signature and typed name.

Tone: courteous, direct and objective; no contractions or slang.

examples.q: Which complimentary close pairs with Dear Sir?

quiz.q: An argumentative essay aims to:
The introduction of an argumentative essay must contain a:
Which is a linking word for addition?
Which linker signals contrast?
Which linker gives a conclusion?
An expository essay is mostly:
A process essay is organised by:
A cause-and-effect essay explains:
options: how one event leads to another

quiz.q: A comparison essay looks at:
A formal letter salutation to an unknown official is:
Complimentary close for Dear Sir/Madam:
Complimentary close for Dear Mr Adamu:
Formal letters avoid:
Which is the correct order for a formal letter?
options: date, writer's address, recipient's address, salutation
writer's address, date, recipient's address, salutation
recipient's address, writer's address, date, salutation
salutation, writer's address, date, recipient's address

quiz.q: Which paragraph of an argumentative essay presents the other side?
A thesis statement is:
options: a one-sentence summary of the writer's position

quiz.q: Which is <u>not</u> a feature of a good argument paragraph?
Which is a strong opening for an argumentative essay?
options: In an era where …, the case for … has never been stronger.
There are many things to say.

quiz.q: Which is the best conclusion?
options: restate thesis + summarise reasons + call to action

quiz.q: Which is a semi-formal letter?
Which of these is <u>not</u> appropriate in a formal letter?
An expository essay 'How to make jollof rice' is organised by:
A definition essay explains:
options: the meaning of a term with examples

quiz.q: A classification essay divides:
Which is a well-chosen thesis for 'Cell phones do more harm than good in schools'?
options: Although cell phones have some benefits, their harm outweighs their good in schools.

quiz.q: Which linker introduces an example?
Which linker shows cause?
Which linker shows result?
Which linker shows time order?
Which is <u>not</u> part of a formal letter?
quiz.solution: Personal greetings belong to informal letters.

quiz.q: An argumentative essay usually has:

options: I write to bring to your attention…

quiz.q: Which best closes a formal complaint letter?
options: I look forward to your prompt response.

quiz.q: A well-structured body paragraph in an argumentative essay follows the pattern:
Which is a fallacy?
options: attacking the person, not the argument (ad hominem)
quiz.solution: Ad hominem is a logical fallacy.

quiz.q: A good expository essay uses tone that is:

A body paragraph should discuss:
options: one clear point supported with evidence

quiz.q: A good expository thesis for 'Effects of climate change in Nigeria' would list:
options: the main effects to be explained

quiz.q: Which is <u>not</u> a linker of consequence?

options: optional and stated in the conclusion if useful

quiz.q: Yours truly is:
options: a valid formal close in American usage
